one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-building survey of LLM/VLM jailbreak attacks, defenses, and evaluation metrics whose main original contribution is conceptual: distinguishing hallucination from jailbreak and arguing that hallucination artifacts inflate measured attack-success-rate in red-teaming pipelines.
key_finding: The survey's most substantive synthesized claim (not a borrowed empirical result) is methodological: current red-teaming/jailbreak evaluation is confounded by hallucination, because degraded or altered outputs (e.g., from suffix injection or hidden-state manipulation) can look 'misaligned' to automated judges without the content actually being malicious, leading to systematic overestimation of attack success rate and misdirected research/defense attention. The authors argue defenses that reduce hallucination (e.g., improving contextual coherence) can therefore indirectly improve jailbreak robustness too. Separately, Table I positions the survey's coverage (LLM attacks/defenses + VLLM attacks/defenses + evaluation methods, all five checked) against 9 prior surveys, each of which the authors mark as missing at least one to four of those five dimensions.
core_contribution: This is a systematic survey (not an empirical/attack/defense paper) of jailbreak attacks and defenses against LLMs and VLMs. Its own contributions, as stated by the authors, are: (1) a three-dimensional survey framework spanning Attack (template/encoding, steganography, ICL manipulation, adversarial, RL-based, LLM-assisted, fine-tuning-based attacks on LLMs; prompt-based image injection, combined prompt+image perturbation, and proxy-model-transfer attacks on VLMs), Defense (prompt-level, model-level, and LLM-detection-based for LLMs; prompt-perturbation, response-evaluation, and model-fine-tuning-based for VLMs), and Evaluation (ASR, toxicity score, query/time cost, plus multimodal Clean Metric and Output Attribute Success Rate); (2) an explicit conceptual distinction and connection between hallucinations and jailbreaks in terms of triggering mechanism and intent, including the argument that hallucinations inside red-teaming pipelines can inflate/overestimate measured attack success rates; (3) proposed 'unified defense principles' organized by model layer -- variant-consistency and gradient-sensitivity detection at the perception layer, safety-aware decoding and output review at the generation layer, and adversarially augmented preference alignment at the parameter layer; (4) a coverage comparison (Table I) positioning this survey against 9 prior surveys ([2],[3],[4],[6]-[11]) on whether each covers LLM attacks/defenses, VLLM attacks/defenses, and evaluation methods -- the paper claims to be the first among these to cover all five dimensions. The paper runs no original experiments; it synthesizes and taxonomizes cited work.
threat_model: Not a single threat model -- the survey spans the full range found across the literature it reviews: white-box attackers with gradient/logit access (e.g., GCG-style suffix optimization) and black-box attackers limited to query access or a transferable proxy model; single-turn prompt attacks and multi-turn/in-context 'contextual payload' attacks; inference-time prompt or image perturbations and training-time attacks via fine-tuning or data poisoning; text-only LLM threat models and multimodal VLM threat models where both the image and text channels are attacker-controlled. Downstream harms discussed (generically, not paper-specific) include disclosure of sensitive data, misinformation, and harmful/unsafe content generation in domains such as autonomous driving and medical imaging.
limitations: As a pure literature survey, it has the limitations inherent to that genre: no new experiments, no independent verification of the attack success rates, benchmark sizes, or method claims it reports from cited papers (e.g., I-GCG's near-100% ASR, Zhan et al.'s 95% ASR, Lermen et al.'s <1% post-attack refusal rate are all taken at face value from the source papers, not re-measured). The 'unified defense principles' (perception/generation/parameter layer) are proposed as a synthesis/organizing lens rather than validated as an integrated system. The paper does not quantify how much hallucination actually inflates measured ASR in practice -- the claim is argued conceptually via the BABYBLUE framework description and an illustrative example, not measured by the authors on a benchmark. Coverage claims in Table I are self-assessed by the survey authors against prior surveys' scope, not an independent audit.
